NAME: ACETAMINOPHEN
COMMENT: has an -OH group on the benzene ring
EXPLANATION: No notable adverse effects at normal dosage.

NAME: PHENACETIN
COMMENT: has an ethoxy group on the benzene ring

NAME: R-IBUPROFEN
EXPLANATION: Inactive form that can be converted in vivo from the distomer to the eutomer, because ibuprofen is used as a racemic mixture.

NAME: S-THALIDOMIDE
EXPLANATION: Has teratogenic activity, although the practical distinction is limited by rapid interconversion between enantiomers.

NAME: TERFENADINE
COMMENT: tert-butyl group on the benzene ring
EXPLANATION: Withdrawn from most markets because of cardiac rhythm risk, especially QT-prolongation-related arrhythmia.

NAME: FEXOFENADINE
COMMENT: one methyl group is replaced with -COOH
EXPLANATION: Terfenadine metabolite without the same toxic liability.

NAME: S-WARFARIN
EXPLANATION: The anticoagulant effect is mainly attributed to the S-isomer, reported to be three to five times more potent than the R-form.

NAME: S-FENOPROFEN
EXPLANATION: The (S)-enantiomer has the desired pharmacological activity, whereas the (R)-isomer is less active; stereoselective bioconversion of (R)-fenoprofen to the (S)-isomer is observed.

NAME: S-KETOPROFEN
EXPLANATION: Most profens are sold as racemic mixtures. For most NSAIDs, pharmacological activity resides in the (S)-enantiomers, whereas the (R)-enantiomers are nearly inactive; many profens, including ketoprofen, undergo unidirectional metabolic chiral inversion from the (R)-acid to the (S)-form.

NAME: ENALAPRIL
EXPLANATION: As a prodrug, enalapril is hydrolyzed in vivo by esterases to the active form, enalaprilat.

NAME: CODEINE
EXPLANATION: Requires hepatic metabolism for activity; part of codeine is converted enzymatically to morphine, which provides analgesia. Codeine is about 10-12 times weaker as an analgesic than morphine.

NAME: MORPHINE
EXPLANATION: Stronger analgesic than codeine; it directly interacts with mu-opioid receptors in the central nervous system and produces pronounced analgesia.

NAME: L-DOPA
COMMENT: terminal carbon bears a carboxyl group (-COOH)
EXPLANATION: Dopamine precursor that requires metabolism to form the active species.

NAME: DOPAMINE
EXPLANATION: Directly stimulates dopamine receptors and acts as a neurotransmitter.

NAME: OSELTAMIVIR
EXPLANATION: Approximately 80% is metabolized to the carboxylate.

NAME: OSELTAMIVIR CARBOXYLATE
EXPLANATION: Active metabolite of the oseltamivir prodrug; formed after administration and acts as an effective selective neuraminidase inhibitor for influenza A and B viruses.

NAME: PREDNISONE
EXPLANATION: Prodrug that is inactive before metabolism.

NAME: SULFASALAZINE
EXPLANATION: Prodrug that is inactive before metabolism.

NAME: HEROIN
EXPLANATION: Higher lipophilicity allows faster blood-brain barrier penetration and faster dependence formation.

NAME: METHADONE
EXPLANATION: Dependence is a key adverse effect, but the drug remains marketed.

NAME: PROPOXYPHENE
EXPLANATION: Cardiotoxic and withdrawn from the market.

NAME: HEROIN
EXPLANATION: Heroin itself has relatively weak affinity for mu-opioid receptors because the 3-hydroxy group required for efficient binding is masked by an acetyl group; overall, heroin acts as a prodrug.

NAME: 6-MAM
EXPLANATION: Highly active heroin metabolite that binds opioid receptors.

NAME: CIPROFLOXACIN
COMMENT: no ethoxy group on the ring
EXPLANATION: Marketed antibiotic with acceptable clinical use.

NAME: GATIFLOXACIN
COMMENT: has an ethoxy group on the ring

NAME: BENZOIC ACID
EXPLANATION: Widely used and not strongly toxic.

NAME: AMPHETAMINE
EXPLANATION: Less active than the paired analogue.

NAME: METHAMPHETAMINE
EXPLANATION: Methylation increases lipophilicity, enabling faster blood-brain barrier penetration and slower metabolism.

NAME: METHANOL
EXPLANATION: Metabolized to formaldehyde, causing blindness and potentially death.

NAME: NAPQI
EXPLANATION: NAPQI becomes toxic when glutathione (GSH) is depleted after acetaminophen overdose.

NAME: OMEPRAZOLE
EXPLANATION: Proton pump inhibitor used as a racemic mixture; the S-enantiomer (esomeprazole) has more predictable pharmacokinetics and lower interindividual variability.

NAME: ESOMEPRAZOLE
EXPLANATION: S-form of omeprazole; single-enantiomer version with higher exposure and more stable metabolism.

NAME: CETIRIZINE
EXPLANATION: Antihistamine used as a mixture of enantiomers.

NAME: THEOPHYLLINE
EXPLANATION: Narrow therapeutic window and stronger toxicity.

NAME: 2-DEOXYGLUCOSE
COMMENT: one -OH group is replaced by H
EXPLANATION: Toxic compound; relatively safe at low doses but may lower blood pressure and slow breathing at high doses.

NAME: PARATHION METHYL
EXPLANATION: Somewhat less toxic and hazardous insecticide.

NAME: METHYL SALICYLATE
EXPLANATION: Can be toxic in overdose, especially in children.

NAME: SODIUM BENZOATE
EXPLANATION: Food additive with relatively low toxicity.

NAME: FLUOROACETATE
EXPLANATION: Highly toxic and blocks the Krebs cycle.

NAME: METHYLENE CHLORIDE
EXPLANATION: Also toxic, but usually less hazardous than chloroform.

NAME: BUPIVACAINE
EXPLANATION: Local anesthetic; racemate where one enantiomer is associated with higher cardiotoxicity.

NAME: KETAMINE
EXPLANATION: Anesthetic used as an enantiomeric mixture.

NAME: OFLOXACIN
EXPLANATION: Quinolone antibiotic used as a racemate.

NAME: ETHINYL ESTRADIOL
EXPLANATION: Synthetic substituted analogue with increased adverse effects.

NAME: METOPROLOL
EXPLANATION: Main active component responsible for beta-blocking activity.

NAME: O-DESMETHYL_METOPROLOL
EXPLANATION: Metabolite that generally lacks clinically meaningful beta-blocking activity.

NAME: VENLAFAXINE
EXPLANATION: Venlafaxine has higher metabolic activity because it is extensively metabolized in the liver by CYP2D6 to an active metabolite.

NAME: DESVENLAFAXINE
EXPLANATION: Desvenlafaxine is already an active metabolite, undergoes less intensive metabolism, and is excreted to a greater extent unchanged.
